text: 1. Bainitic Transformation Kinetics: Steel S5 exhibited the fastest bainitic transformation ( Bt: 700–1000 s), whereas S3 (with the highest Al content ≈8 wt.%) was the slowest (Bt: 14,000–43,000 s, depending on austempering temperature). In S3, the presence of ${ \mathfrak { X } } _ { \mathfrak { p } }$ promotes local carbon enrichment and stabilization of surrounding austenite, which impedes bainite nucleation and growth and explains its slowest transformation. Steels S1 and S2 fell into an intermediate-fast range (Bt: 1600–4000 s), and Ti-micro-alloyed S4 showed initially rapid nucleation but was slowed by bainitic sheaf impingement (Bt: 3000–3500 s). Notably, the transformation time was not affected by a small Ni addition (0.78 wt.% in S4 and S5).

2. UTS-El (PSE) Performance: Despite compositional differences, several alloys achieved high combinations of UTS and El. Ti-alloyed S4 attained the best product of strength and elongation (PSE ≈ 13.6 GPa⋅%) under optimal conditions $\mathrm { ( a t T _ { A } }$ of 1150 ◦C, at $\mathrm { T } _ { \mathrm { B } } \mathrm { o f } 3 5 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ) .$ . Alloy S2 achieved a similar peak performance (PSE ≈ 13.0 GPa⋅% at $\mathrm { { T _ { B } } }$ of $3 5 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } )$ . In contrast, S3 at a low austempering temperature $( 2 7 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } )$ exhibited a severe loss of ductility $( \mathrm { E l } \approx 2 . 4 \% )$ , leading to a minimum PSE of only ≈ 3.5 GPa⋅%. S5, despite its rapid bainite formation, showed moderate strength–ductility values, with PSE in the range of ≈10.7–11.5 GPa⋅%.

3. Precipitation Characteristics: Primary carbides of type $( \mathrm { F e , M ) _ { x } C _ { y } }$ were widely observed in steels S1, S2, and S3 without any associated micro-void formation. These precipitates had negligible direct influence on tensile properties, consistent with thermodynamic simulations indicating their formation occurred above the bainitic hold temperature. Their stabilization is attributed to rapid cooling and the presence of residual elements (e.g. Mo, Si) in the particles, supporting the interpretation that they are primarily formed during prior processing rather than during austempering.

4. Effect of Ti Microalloying: In the S4 steel, Ti additions led to the coarsening of MoC into more complex TiMoC precipitates, predominantly formed during above austempering temperatures. No new secondary precipitates were detected during austempering (within SEM resolution) as also evident from TTP calculation. The presence of Ti-rich carbides suppressed the formation of more deleterious Fe3C or unmodified MoC particles and promoted grain refinement. However, S4 exhibited similar bainitic transformation kinetics and a slightly improved UTS–El balance relative to the steels S1 and S2.

5. Lower $\underline { { \mathbf { C } } } \underline { { - } } \underline { { \mathbf { A l } } }$ alloying approach: The ${ \mathrm { l o w } } { \mathrm { - } } { \mathrm { C } } ,$ moderate-Al alloy S5 developed a fine, uniform bainitic microstructure with minimal sheaf impingement. When austempered near its Ms $( \mathrm { T } _ { \mathrm { B } } \colon 3 3 5 \ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ , Ms: 331 ${ } ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ) ,$ S5 showed very narrow bainitic-ferrite lath packets with only sparse lenticular martensite and localized misorientation hot-spots at lath/martensite interfaces. This refined microstructure facilitated a very short transformation time (700 s, the fastest among all steels). Under these conditions, S5 achieved high strength (UTS: 1530 MPa) albeit at the expense of ductility (El: 7 %). # Author contributions Serkan Turan: conceptualization, investigation, formal analysis, methodology, software, validation, visualization, writing – original draft, writing – review & editing. Johannes Buhl: supervision, formal analysis, writing – review & editing. Heinz Palkowski: conceptualization, formal analysis, funding acquisition, project administration, supervision, writing – review & editing. # Data availability statement The data that support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request. # Declaration of competing interest The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper. # Acknowledgement Funded by the Deutsche Forschungsgemeinschaft (DFG, German Research Foundation) – Project Number: 451823328.
